summary: DeVault explains his approach to managing dotfiles by making his entire home directory a git repository, rather than using specialized tools like GNU stow. The trick is a one-byte .gitignore file containing just '*', which ignores everything by default and requires explicit git add -f to track specific files. He extends this with per-host customization using hostname-based PATH directories and config includes. The approach leverages familiar Unix primitives and git knowledge rather than requiring specialized tooling.
key_points: A single-character .gitignore ('*') makes the system work by ignoring everything unless explicitly force-added
Once a file is tracked via git add -f, it behaves normally in git workflows without needing the force flag again
Per-host customization is achieved through hostname-based bin directories and config includes, handling different machines and architectures
The $HOME directory doubles as a Unix-style prefix with ~/bin, ~/lib, allowing wrapper scripts to shadow system programs
The approach composes existing Unix primitives and git rather than introducing specialized dotfile management tools
money_quotes: The key to making this work well is my one-byte .gitignore file.
A quick log-out and back in and I feel right at $HOME.
Replacing the needlessly annoying-to-customize xdg-open with one that just does what I want, falling back to /usr/bin/xdg-open if necessary.
Rather than using (and learning) any specialized tools, I find my needs quite adequately satisfied by a simple composition of several Unix primitives with a tool I'm already very familiar with: git.
Version controlling your configuration files is a desirable trait even with other systems, so why not ditch the middleman?
key_insight: Managing dotfiles doesn't require specialized tools — a git repository with a wildcard .gitignore and Unix conventions for per-host customization is simpler and more composable.
